steps.title: Identify each text’s critique of Tuchman
steps.content: Text 1 argues that Tuchman failed to address Eastern European events before the war, which caused her to overstate Germany’s role as the war’s instigator — a flaw in the scope of her research leading to a wrong interpretation. Text 2 argues that her main thesis — that powers locked themselves into catastrophe by refusing to deviate from pre-war military plans — is “implausibly reductive,” meaning overly simplistic.

steps.title: Match each critique to the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The author of Text 1 argues that Tuchman should have relied more on the work of other historians, while the author of Text 2 implies that Tuchman's most interesting claims result from her original research.: Text 1’s summary is accurate, but Text 2 never says her most interesting claims come from original research. ✗
  • Choice B — The author of Text 1 believes that the scope of Tuchman's research led her to an incorrect interpretation, while the author of Text 2 believes that Tuchman's central argument is overly simplistic.: both halves match the texts exactly. ✓
  • Choice C — The author of Text 1 asserts that the writing style of The Guns of August makes it worthwhile to read despite any perceived deficiency in Tuchman's research, while the author of Text 2 focuses exclusively on the weakness of Tuchman's interpretation of events.: Text 1 never says the book is worthwhile to read despite its flaws, and Text 2 actually praises Tuchman’s analysis of primary sources. ✗
  • Choice D — The author of Text 1 claims that Tuchman would agree that World War I was largely due to events in Eastern Europe, while the author of Text 2 maintains that Tuchman would say that Eastern European leaders were not committed to military plans in the same way that other leaders were.: neither text makes these claims. ✗
That matches Choice B — The author of Text 1 believes that the scope of Tuchman's research led her to an incorrect interpretation, while the author of Text 2 believes that Tuchman's central argument is overly simplistic..
